summary: Parallel web research sweep to find high-pain, underserved reader problems and book market gaps

ranked.problem: Parents have no credible, practical trade book on teens' emotional (sometimes romantic) attachments to AI companion chatbots — how to detect it, talk about it, set house rules, and intervene without driving the kid deeper in. The 'Anxious Generation' moment for AI companions has no 'Anxious Generation' book.
ranked.audience: Parents of the ~42M US kids aged 8-17 — 72% of teens have used AI companions, 52% regularly (Common Sense Media national survey, n=1,060, NORC). This is the exact demographic that kept Haidt's 'The Anxious Generation' at #1 NYT for over a year, plus a schools/PTA bulk-buy channel.
ranked.urgency: Stacked 2025-26 catalysts: Common Sense Media 'unacceptable risk' verdict (July 2025); Stanford risk assessment showing companions producing sexual content and self-harm encouragement with teen personas (Aug 2025); Character.AI banning under-18 open chat (Nov 2025); the Character.AI/Google teen-suicide settlements (Jan 2026); California SB 243 effective Jan 1, 2026; MIT Tech Review naming AI companions a 2026 Breakthrough Technology. Parental awareness is exploding faster than book-length guidance exists.
ranked.evidence_summary: THREE independent scouts (forum-pain, retail-gaps, search-demand) converged on this with non-overlapping evidence: the quantified national survey (72%/52%, 33% choosing AI over humans for serious conversations); an arXiv study finding teen self-reports in addiction language ('withdrawal, tolerance, relapse'); documented parental unawareness (Child Mind, Children and Screens); litigation/legislation catalysts; and a directly observed Amazon shelf of KDP spam ('BLOCKBUSTER STORY', 'KITS FOR LIFE' as author names). The flood of stopgap web guides (APA, PolitiFact, KQED, UNICEF, Child Mind) is itself the demand signal — articles are filling a book-shaped hole.
ranked.gap: Haidt (2024) predates companion bots and covers phones/social media; De Kai's 'Raising AI' (MIT Press) is philosophy about humanity parenting AI, not a how-to; everything else is verified KDP/AI-generated spam. Three scouts independently searched for a mainstream-publisher parent guide to companion-bot attachment and found none — the gap is 'Anxious Generation' rigor plus 'How to Talk So Kids Will Listen' scripts, specifically for synthetic relationships.
ranked.rationale: Strongest cross-validation in the entire set (3 of 7 scouts, three different research angles, all high-confidence), a proven ferocious comp-title buyer base, quantified mainstream behavior, legal/legislative news hooks that will keep regenerating coverage through 2026-27, and a verifiably empty credible shelf. It is the topical pick, but the evidence quality and convergence justify it — the concept is only 'obvious' in hindsight; the shelf proves no one has executed it.
ranked.risks: Big-5 pipeline risk is real and unannounced deals are invisible — Haidt, a Common Sense-affiliated author, or a journalist on the Character.AI litigation beat could preempt. Speed to proposal matters more here than anywhere else on this list. Mitigation: the practical-scripts positioning (detection, conversations, house rules) differs from the diagnosis-heavy book a journalist would write.

ranked.problem: Tens of millions of Americans are being forced OFF GLP-1 drugs by insurance cuts and cost, face documented rapid weight regain plus an unaddressed psychological aftermath ('Ozempic personality', returning food noise, body dysmorphia, reshaped relationships) — and there is no definitive big-publisher book on life after the drug: tapering, maintenance, identity, and the restart decision.
ranked.audience: 1 in 8 US adults currently take a GLP-1 and 18% have ever taken one (KFF) — 30M+ people skewing women 50-64, the core health/self-help buying demographic, already conditioned to spend $900-1,400/month on the problem. The forced-off subset is enormous: 41M commercially insured people now have zero Wegovy coverage (+42% YoY) and 109M lost Zepbound coverage (GoodRx via NPR).
ranked.urgency: Coverage collapse is happening in real time (BCBS MA/MI, Harvard Pilgrim, HCA, NC Medicaid all dropped coverage effective 2026; CNBC 7/8/2026 — yesterday relative to today's date — confirms employers are not expanding). Jan 2026 Cambridge analysis: most weight regained within ~18 months of stopping, only ~25% of losses kept. The first mass user cohorts are now 2-3 years in, hitting maintenance or forced discontinuation simultaneously.
ranked.evidence_summary: TWO independent scouts (retail-gaps, demographic-shifts) converged with complementary halves: one documented the forced-discontinuation crisis and the shelf (HarperCollins' 'Ozempic Revolution' is on-ramp only, reviewers call it beginner-level; the sole credible off-ramp title is a weeks-old, narrowly framed small-press book); the other documented the psychological aftermath demand (KFF/Gallup scale data, PMC regain meta-analysis, 'Ozempic personality' explainer wave, therapist essays saying the clinical conversation misses the psychological side). Merged, they define one book: the definitive medical + psychological guide to coming off, staying off, or restarting.
ranked.gap: The category leader is on-ramp focused; the only off-ramp title is brand-new, small-press, 10-week-program framed, and per coverage omits identity/relationships/body image; the rest is KDP keyword squatting. No big-publisher book covers the whole forced-discontinuation reality: taper vs. cold stop, muscle preservation, food-noise return, gray-market temptation, cost triage, the restart decision, AND who you are after the drug.
ranked.urgency_note: window open but visibly closing (BenBella Dec 2025, Penguin's 'Weightless' in pipeline)
ranked.rationale: Two-scout convergence, the hardest demand numbers on the list (KFF/Gallup/GoodRx/Cambridge), a genuinely acute involuntary crisis (people are being pushed off the drug, not choosing), and a buyer already spending four figures monthly on this exact problem. The merged medical+psychological framing is more original and defensible than either scout's half alone.
ranked.risks: Fastest-closing window in the set — publishers have clearly noticed. Requires a credentialed author (MD or MD+journalist pair) for credibility. Drug-landscape churn (oral GLP-1s, price cuts) could soften the forced-off dynamic, though regain science stays relevant regardless.

ranked.problem: Spiritually curious Gen Z adults — the cohort driving Bible sales to a 21-year high — have no mainstream, culturally fluent, non-preachy trade book explaining Christianity from zero for someone raised entirely secular. The books still handed to seekers are from 1952, 1998, and 2008.
ranked.audience: Adults 18-28 showing publisher-documented 'renewed curiosity about faith': ~19M Bibles sold in the US in 2025 (2x 2019), with Tyndale and HarperCollins attributing growth to Gen Z first-time buyers; US Bible users rose 38%→41% in one year; religion category unit sales ~67M (+5.4%) in an otherwise flat print market. This cohort is paying for religion books right now, transatlantically (UK Bible sales nearly doubled 2019-2024).
ranked.urgency: Publishers explicitly report demand for 'accessible formats designed for first-time readers' outrunning supply — the on-ramp shortage is named by the industry itself. Religion was one of the few growth categories of 2025; the cultural moment (post-deconstruction curiosity, meaning-hunger visible in +14.7% self-help) is live now.
ranked.evidence_summary: Single scout but the hardest industry data in the set: Circana unit sales, multi-year trend lines, named publisher testimony, and a competitive scan showing the seeker canon is 18-74 years old (Mere Christianity 1952, Case for Christ 1998, Reason for God 2008) with recent entrants either small-press men-only framing or KDP 'Gen Z Bible' guides. Demand is proven by actual purchases (Bibles), not surveys or discourse.
ranked.gap: A 'Mere Christianity for the algorithm-raised' — big-trade, culturally fluent, zero-assumed-knowledge — does not exist. Everything current is church-facing, apologetics-press, or downmarket KDP. The gap is between demonstrated first-time-buyer demand (Bibles) and the absence of the companion explainer those buyers need next.
ranked.rationale: Ranks this high because it is the most ORIGINAL commercially validated pick: no other scout was circling it, demand is measured in units sold rather than media noise, and mainstream publishers are structurally slow here (big-5 trade and Christian-market presses talk past each other, which is why the lane stays open). Exactly the non-obvious signal this exercise is designed to surface.
ranked.risks: Execution is entirely author-dependent — this needs a young, credible adult-convert writer with literary chops, not just the right topic; a mismatched author produces another apologetics book. Cultural-moment sales spikes (e.g., post-Kirk-assassination) may prove partly event-driven.

ranked.problem: AI voice-cloning and deepfake scams are draining seniors' savings at record scale — and the canonical consumer scam-defense book (Abagnale, 2019) predates consumer generative AI entirely. Families need a protocol book (safe words, verification rituals, money holds) for the AI fraud era.
ranked.audience: 62M Americans 65+ plus their Gen-X/millennial adult children — the actual buyers, in a classic 'buy it for Mom' dual-purchase structure. Seniors and their kids are among the most reliable print-book demographics; AARP's fraud-watch audience alone is tens of millions.
ranked.urgency: AI scams up 1,210% in 2025 (Vectra); deepfake vishing up 1,600%+ in a single quarter; cybercrime losses among 60+ hit ~$7.7B in 2025 (+37% YoY); FBI IC3 logged 22,000+ AI-fraud complaints with $893M adjusted losses; voice cloning now needs 3 seconds of audio. CNN, Consumer Reports, AARP, and local TV are all running protect-yourself explainers in mid-2026 — mass pain, mass coverage, no current book.
ranked.evidence_summary: Single scout, high confidence: enormous quantified losses from multiple institutional sources (FBI, Vectra, Consumer Reports, NCOA), relentless mid-2026 news coverage, and a verified shelf where the 7-year-old pre-generative-AI incumbent is the category leader and the only AI-era entrants are ISBN-979 self-pub. NCOA publishing a web guide 'because no book exists' is a direct demand signal. Scout honestly flags that dollar figures vary across sources and need careful sourcing.
ranked.gap: No major publisher has fielded an AI-era family fraud-defense book despite billions in documented losses. The 'family safe word' protocol is currently owned by TV news segments — a book could own it the way 'The Anxious Generation' owns phone-free schools. Must be framed as evergreen protocols, not scam-of-the-month taxonomy.
ranked.rationale: High pain intensity (life-savings losses), a dual-buyer gift/duty purchase structure that de-risks conversion, quantified urgency, and a stale named incumbent. More original than the AI-career cluster because nobody else in publishing discourse is treating this as a book category yet — it is being treated as a news beat.
ranked.risks: Scam tactics evolve fast, so a tactics-focused book dates in 18 months — the protocols-not-products framing is load-bearing. Statistical sourcing is inconsistent across outlets and would need rigorous verification. AARP itself could publish the definitive version through its own imprint channel.

ranked.problem: Parents of autistic/IDD teens hit the age 18-22 'services cliff' — school-based supports vanish overnight — and every planning book on the shelf became factually obsolete on January 1, 2026, when the ABLE Age Adjustment Act rewrote the single most important savings vehicle (eligibility to onset-before-46, ~6M newly eligible).
ranked.audience: Parents of ~50,000 autistic youths finishing US high school each year (~500K transitioning to adulthood over the next decade), plus the wider IDD parent population — proven buyers of a large special-needs parenting shelf who also pay for attorneys, advocates, and conferences; nonprofits and parent centers bulk-buy for workshops, a built-in institutional channel.
ranked.urgency: A double hard catalyst: the first mass wave of the early-2000s autism diagnosis surge is aging into adulthood right now, and the Jan 1, 2026 ABLE expansion is a dated regulatory event that invalidates all pre-2026 guidance. Regulatory why-nows are rarer and more durable than discourse why-nows.
ranked.evidence_summary: Single scout, high confidence, multi-institutional: Drexel's Life Course Outcomes program documents the cliff; Child Mind and Kennedy Krieger document parent unpreparedness; ABLE NRC and Disability Belongs confirm the 2026 rule change; multiple parent orgs sustain entire content verticals on this transition (blog posts filling a book-shaped hole). Shelf verified dated/fragmented: 2012, 2018, a 2021 nonprofit PDF, and legal-only titles.
ranked.gap: Nothing current integrates the 2026 ABLE rules, supported decision-making (the modern guardianship alternative), Medicaid waiver waitlist strategy, and employment/housing into one parent-facing trade book. The demand is currently served by scattered org blog verticals.
ranked.rationale: The most defensible niche on the list: desperate, organized, proven-paying audience; institutional bulk channel; a hard regulatory expiry date on all competition; and near-zero big-publisher attention, meaning low preemption risk — the opposite profile of the AI-topic entries. Smaller ceiling than ranks 1-4 but the highest probability of owning its category for a decade, like 'The 36-Hour Day' did.
ranked.risks: Audience is smaller than mass-market picks (this is a perennial-backlist play, not a bestseller play). State-by-state Medicaid variation makes a single national book hard — needs a framework-plus-worksheets structure. Scout's page-level evidence came from search extracts due to fetch blocks.

ranked.problem: Adult children — increasingly millennials with toddlers — are ambushed by boomer-parent decline (hospital discharge, dementia, POA, Medicaid, a parent who refuses help, sibling warfare) and the category 'bible' is a 650-page tome last revised in 2014, while the 'difficult parent' standard is from 1999. No current, triage-structured 'what do I do this week' field guide exists.
ranked.audience: 63M US family caregivers — 1 in 4 US adults, up ~20M since 2015 (AARP/NAC July 2025) — skewing women 40-60, the single strongest nonfiction-buying demographic. Pew (Feb 2026, n=8,750): 24% of adults with a living parent 65+ are caregiving; 47% of caregivers under 50 are simultaneously raising children. Caregivers spend ~$10K/year out of pocket; a $28 book is trivial.
ranked.urgency: 2026 is 'Peak 80': the first boomers turn 80 this year; AARP's July 2025 report is literally titled a 'crisis point'; the caregiver support ratio is collapsing (6.1→4.1 per elder by 2030); 24-hour home care runs ~$290K/year; new mechanisms the old books don't cover (Medicaid self-direction paid-family-caregiver waivers, post-2014 Medicare changes, telehealth, portal-based care coordination) now exist.
ranked.evidence_summary: THREE independent scouts converged (forum-pain, retail-gaps, demographic-shifts) with complementary evidence: macro demographics (Fortune, AARP PPI, Washington Times), the strongest shelf autopsy in the set (Virginia Morris's 500K-copy 'Caregivers' Bible' verified 12 years stale with Goodreads reviewers calling it 'overwhelming'; the 1999 difficult-parent standard criticized for blaming the adult child), and fresh 2025-26 national studies (Pew's first dedicated caregiving report, AARP's 45% caregiving jump). One scout honestly noted thin direct forum-quote evidence; the other two backfilled with named-study demand data.
ranked.gap: Incumbents are authoritative but 12-27 years stale, encyclopedic rather than triage-structured, dementia-only, or memoir. Missing: a current-rules (2026), crisis-sequenced navigation guide covering paid-family-caregiver programs, the refusing-help parent, distributed siblings, and modern logistics — in a millennial/Gen-X voice.
ranked.rationale: Triple-scout convergence with verified shelf staleness on a 500K-copy category is a legitimate commercial signal even though the topic is evergreen — this is the 'safest' entry I kept, ranked mid-list deliberately. It survived the obviousness filter because the gap is executional (nobody has refreshed the category leader), not conceptual, and evergreen demand plus a 2026 news hook is a durable combination.
ranked.risks: Category is crowded-with-dated-titles rather than empty; Jennifer Levin's 'Generation Care' (2025) claims adjacent millennial territory; Workman could simply commission Morris's 4th edition and close the gap overnight. Differentiation must be structural (triage format) not just newer.

ranked.problem: The classes of 2025-2027 graduated into the collapse of the entry-level rung — AI absorbed the grunt work that was the traditional training ground — and every career book assumes a first rung that no longer exists. Nothing serious answers the structural question grads actually ask: how do you build skills, credibility, and identity when the apprenticeship layer is gone?
ranked.audience: ~4M US degree completions/year plus parents (graduation-gift books are a perennial category — an acute, time-boxed buying occasion like test prep), plus university career centers as an institutional channel. Pain is self-aware and loud: r/csMajors, r/jobs, r/recruitinghell saturated with 200+-applications-no-reply threads.
ranked.urgency: 43% of grads 22-27 underemployed (worst since the pandemic); entry-level postings down ~35% since early 2023, junior tech postings down up to 67%; companies adopting genAI cut entry-level hiring ~80% (Forbes, May 2026); NACE: AI-skill demand in entry-level jobs nearly tripled since fall 2025. Multiple analysts call it a permanent structural break, and it is the dinner-table topic for 2026-27 graduating classes.
ranked.evidence_summary: TWO independent scouts (forum-pain, search-demand) converged with distinct source sets — one from community pain and labor-market reports (Rezi, Extern, IEEE, NACE, Entrepreneur's AI-vs-AI application arms race), one from 2026 business-press data (Forbes, Washington Monthly, Fortune's gig-work escape valve, The Up and Up's 'application-maxxing' reporting). Both independently verified the shelf: 'Parachute' still sells a 1970 framework with AI bolted on; Mollick is for established professionals; the rest is prompt-writing ebooks.
ranked.gap: No trade book addresses career-building when the bottom rung is structurally gone — demand is met by Forbes listicles, Substacks, and TikTok career coaches. A 'What Color Is Your Parachute'-scale opening for a post-ladder playbook (apprenticeship substitutes, small-business and gig routes, proof-of-work portfolios, AI leverage without commodity sameness).
ranked.rationale: Cross-validated, enormous audience with a built-in gift occasion, and genuinely empty shelf — but ranked below less-obvious picks because this is the single most media-saturated topic in the set, which means the heaviest invisible pipeline: agented titles for 2027 are near-certain. Wins only on depth of reporting and framework originality, not on being first.
ranked.risks: Highest preemption risk on the list after #1. Also a moving target: if the 2027 labor market partially recovers, the 'permanent break' thesis weakens and the book reads as recession artifact. Needs a reported, structural angle rather than tips to survive.

ranked.problem: Singles fleeing dating apps — an exodus documented in Match Group's and Bumble's own financials — have no idea how to meet people offline; an entire generation never learned to flirt, approach, or build romantic connection in person, and the category-defining dating book was written by Hinge's own scientist for the app era.
ranked.audience: Gen Z and younger-millennial singles (~20-40), skewing female — the strongest self-help buying segment, with proven category behavior (Logan Ury's app-era book was a bestseller). Match Group paid users down 5% YoY to 14.2M and Bumble cut 30% of staff, so tens of millions of ex-app users exist in the US alone; IRL singles-events businesses (Thursday, CitySwoon) are growing on the demand.
ranked.urgency: The exodus became industry-wide fact in 2025-26: Match cut 13% of workforce citing Gen Z decline; 75%+ of Gen Z report app burnout (highest of any cohort); a former Hinge content lead publicly calls the apps 'doomed.' NPR, Fortune, Columbia News Service all note people want offline connection but lack the skills — instruction demand currently met by articles, Substacks, and coaches.
ranked.evidence_summary: Single scout, high confidence, with unusually hard evidence for a lifestyle topic: company financials and layoffs (not just vibes), a named survey on burnout, service journalism explicitly teaching IRL flirting (NPR), and a competitive scan finding the incumbent ('How to Not Die Alone', 2021) app-optimized to the point that reviewers flag its dated Hinge jokes, with no publisher-backed post-app title found.
ranked.gap: A behavioral-science-grounded 'how to date after the apps' book has no incumbent — the space is held by Substacks, coach blogs, and listicles. The angle inverts the entire existing dating-book canon, which assumes the apps as infrastructure.
ranked.rationale: Earns the last slot over flashier AI-work concepts because it is original (nobody else in publishing is treating the app exodus as a book category), the demand evidence is corporate-financial rather than discourse-based, the audience has proven category buying behavior, and preemption risk looks low. A clean counter-trend concept with a multi-year runway as app decline compounds.
ranked.risks: Author platform matters enormously in dating self-help — this likely needs a coach/researcher with existing audience. Risk that the exodus partially reverses with AI-matchmaking product cycles, or that the book market treats this as magazine-feature material rather than a book-length problem.

result.discarded: Strong candidates cut, and why: (1) AI COGNITIVE ATROPHY / 'Deep Work for the LLM era' (forum-pain) — vivid first-person pain (HN, Microsoft Research, Slashdot) and a real gap, but the framing is instantly preemptable by Cal Newport or any established attention author, the scout could not verify quotes beyond search extracts, and the concept is the most 'obvious next book' in the AI-anxiety space; it lost the last slot to offline dating on originality and preemption risk. (2) AI CAREER-IDENTITY GRIEF + MID-CAREER AI SURVIVAL (two scouts, merged) — genuine emerging pain ('professional identity purgatory', 600+-reply Reddit threads), but evidence is essay/discourse-heavy rather than behavioral, the two versions pull in different directions (grief memoir vs. reskilling playbook), one scout self-rated low-to-medium, and the trend's visibility (Fortune/Forbes covers this month) implies heavy pipeline; already partially represented via rank 7. (3) MIDLIFE-HEROINE ROMANTASY (social-reading) — the single best fiction signal (editors literally aging heroines down is structural supply blockage), but fiction concepts carry 18-24-month timing risk, the key RWA survey is from 2017, and success depends on landing a specific novel, not a concept; flagged as the top fiction wildcard if the project wants one. (4) LOW-SPICE 'YEARNING' SECULAR ROMANCE and HORRORMANCE/FEMGORE-ROMANCE (three scout entries, two merged) — real reader-built-database pain and named editor demand, but publishers are visibly already acquiring here (Baker's Haven imprint; 'major breakout' predictions), making these trend-surfing rather than gap-filling. (5) YOUNG MEN NOT READING FICTION (two scouts converged) — the convergence is on industry discourse, not reader pain; key stats contested by Vox; both scouts flag the fatal flaw that the target buyer under-buys books by definition; conversion risk unpriceable. (6) MALE FRIENDSHIP RECESSION — strong verified stats but saturated 'male loneliness epidemic' discourse, thin shelf scan, and the gap claim rests on absence-of-evidence. (7) GEN Z BRAIN ROT / ANALOG LIVING — great retail timing signal (Circana's 'analog living sections') but the digital-wellness shelf is the most crowded adjacent category in nonfiction; voice-only differentiation is too weak a moat, and the 63% stat is from a low-authority source. (8) SOLO AGERS — AARP itself notes the shelf is 'expanding'; quality/currency gap only. (9) POST-SURFSIDE HOA/CONDO BOARDS — wonderfully specific and urgent but Florida-concentrated with unproven individual book-buying (boards lean on management companies); best as a niche-press play. (10) BOOMER BUSINESS EXIT — good-but-dated incumbents make this a relative gap in a category already saturated with advisor-funnel books. (11) FRACTIONAL EXECUTIVE PLAYBOOK — demand stats come from marketplaces incentivized to hype, and the self-pub/course competition is denser than the ideal gap; works only with a platform author, which makes it an author bet, not a concept bet.
method_notes: Ran ~18 WebSearch queries across money, career, parenting, relationships, health, caregiving, education, and technology-anxiety domains, using pain-phrasing and site-targeted queries. Key constraint: the environment's egress gateway policy-denied every full-page fetch (WebFetch and curl both got 403 CONNECT rejections for reddit, arxiv, HN, CNBC, MIT Tech Review, Fortune, etc.; Apify fetch tool required unavailable approval), so I could not read pages in full — all evidence comes from detailed search-result extracts, each tied to a real URL surfaced in those searches. I flagged this in per-opportunity confidence. Very narrow quoted-phrase Reddit queries mostly returned nothing; broader topical queries worked. Dead ends explored and dropped: perimenopause (now well served — recent bestsellers), Gen Z BNPL debt (real pain but crowded personal-finance shelf, no clear book-shaped gap), GLP-1 food-identity (searches came up dry), adult AI-partner betrayal/grief as a standalone book (vivid pain — r/MyBoyfriendIsAI, GPT-4o shutdown grief covered by MIT Tech Review — but the space already has James Muldoon's 'Love Machines' and the buying audience is unproven), parent estrangement (well covered by Coleman 2021), teacher AI-cheating crisis (acute but teachers' pain is institutional; folded partly into the parenting opportunity). Amazon/Goodreads competitive checks were done via search for each surviving candidate; the four reported all show dated, self-published, or merely tangential competition.

Ran 14 distinct WebSearch queries across: Circana BookScan 2025 year-end and Q1/Q3 category data, Publishers Weekly financial reporting, 2026 trend coverage (Circana, BookBrowse, Book Riot), agent wish-lists (#MSWL, agent Substacks), the male-reader debate (Bookseller, Boston Globe, Republic of Letters, Conduit Books coverage), Bible/religion sales boom (RNS, Christianity Today, Christian Today), Anxious Generation follow-on landscape, brain rot/analog living (Nat Geo, PW Winter Institute 2026), AI-and-work book lists, and competitive checks (Goodreads digital-detox shelf, Amazon listings, skeptic-Christianity titles, books-for-young-men recommendation threads). IMPORTANT CONSTRAINT: the sandbox's outbound proxy denied CONNECT (403) to virtually every publisher/news domain, so full-page WebFetch/curl reads all failed; every claim comes from substantive content returned inside search results tied to the listed URLs, not from full-page reads — treat exact figures as needing one confirmation pass before publication use. Hard numbers that recurred across multiple independent results (print +0.3% 2025; self-help +14.7%; romance +3.9%/44M; fantasy -8.7%/24.1M; religion +5.4%/~67M; Bibles 19M units, +12%, 21-year high; Anxious Generation 2M+ copies) are the most reliable. Dead ends: gender/age demographic splits for self-help buyers (Circana doesn't publish them); Amazon direct browsing (blocked); Jane Friedman and Publishers Lunch full articles (403). Deliberately dropped: Anxious Generation parent-guide follow-on (gap already filled by Haidt's own 2026 kids' book and '10 Rules for Raising Kids in a High-Tech World') and perimenopause (post-Haver shelf now crowded).

Ran ~15 distinct web searches across candidate categories (eldercare, GLP-1, kids+AI companions, male friendship, estrangement, gray divorce, AI career reinvention, Anxious Generation follow-ons). Direct page fetches were largely blocked in this environment: WebFetch and curl returned 403 via the agent proxy for Goodreads, Amazon, NPR, TechCrunch, AARP, MUSC, healthychildren.org, and Common Sense Media, so page content and review quotes were extracted through the search tool's page-reading (which returned substantial verbatim content from Goodreads reviews, Amazon listings, publisher pages, and news articles). All quoted review complaints (Morris 'overwhelming,' Sowa 'very simple/nothing new,' Lebow 'too much responsibility on adult children,' Dickins '£2500 stag weekend') came from search-extracted Goodreads/Amazon content tied to the listed URLs. Came up dry / rejected: family estrangement (well-served: Coleman 2021, Pillemer 2020, Gilbertson, Sharon Martin — no clean gap), gray divorce (Nolo keeps 'Divorce After 50' in updated editions; multiple credible mid-list titles), AI career-survival books (crowded: Mollick, dozens of 2025-2026 lists), phone-free-childhood implementation (adjacent titles emerging). Known limitation: could not load full Goodreads review pages or Amazon review tabs directly, so star-distribution and complaint-frequency counts are based on surfaced snippets rather than exhaustive review reads; flagged in confidence fields where this matters.

Ran 13 distinct WebSearch queries across the assigned angle (search-demand signals, trend roundups, Substack/newsletter niches, then demand-and-competition verification per candidate). IMPORTANT LIMITATION: every full-page WebFetch attempt (CNBC, Stanford, APA, Child Mind, NPR, Amazon, Goodreads, Washington Monthly) was blocked with HTTP 403 by the environment's egress proxy (confirmed via proxy status: 'gateway answered 403 to CONNECT — policy denial'), and the Apify remote-browser MCP tool required an approval this subagent couldn't obtain. So evidence comes from WebSearch result snippets/summaries (which were substantive and dated) rather than full-page reads; all URLs listed were surfaced and summarized by search, not read in full. What worked: pairing a demand search ('X rising 2025/2026 statistics') with a competition search ('X book amazon 2025 publisher') per candidate reliably separated real gaps from saturated ones. Dry holes / rejected candidates: GLP-1/Ozempic books — market already saturated in 2025-26 (Weightless, The Ozempic Revolution, After Ozempic, Off the Scales), no gap; generic Google Trends roundups (fibermaxxing, butter yellow) — fads, not book problems; Substack top-category scan (politics/health/tech) — incumbents already have books; 'why is there no book about X' Reddit query returned nothing. Ranked opportunities 1-3 (grad career collapse, parents' AI-companion guide, AI-era scam defense) all share the same structural finding: heavy 2026 news coverage + institutional stopgap web guides + only self-published or pre-AI incumbent books. Solo aging included at medium confidence as the fourth.

Ran 13 distinct WebSearch queries across the assigned candidate shifts (GLP-1/food culture, millennial eldercare, AI career identity, gray divorce, dating-app exodus, male loneliness) plus competitive-landscape searches (Amazon/Goodreads titles for each candidate). Full-page WebFetch was blocked by the environment's proxy policy (403 CONNECT denial on fortune.com, npr.org, pewresearch.org, kff.org, healthline.com, etc.), and the Apify remote-browser MCP tool required an approval unavailable in this subagent context — so evidence comes from detailed search-result extracts with real URLs rather than full-page reads; stats quoted are as reported in those extracts. What worked: pairing a demand search (venting/stats) with a dedicated book-landscape search per candidate; this killed one candidate (male loneliness — heavy discourse but the book angle is muddier: Reiner, Bamiro, and adjacent friendship titles already exist, and the pain-haver is famously not a self-help buyer) and weakened another (millennial eldercare — Jennifer Levin's 2025 'Generation Care' partially fills it; flagged honestly in that entry). Came up dry on: a defining post-app dating book (searched twice — only Substacks and coach blogs), and any book on GLP-1 psychological aftermath (only user guides and a quit-the-meds playbook). Did not investigate climate migration, childfree life, or post-religious meaning-making in depth due to fetch constraints; those remain unexplored. Confidence ratings reflect that some second-hand stats (e.g., 45% living-standard drop, Forbes Health burnout survey) were seen in search extracts but not verified at the primary source.

Ran 10 distinct WebSearch queries across: BookTok 2026 trend coverage, romantasy fatigue/saturation, AO3 2025 tag rankings, low-spice/closed-door romance demand, femgore/female-rage horror, horror-romance hybrids, midlife heroines/paranormal women's fiction, seasoned romance, Gen Z print-buying stats, standalone-fantasy demand, 'yearning' trend data, and the men-don't-read-fiction discourse. IMPORTANT LIMITATION: direct page fetches (WebFetch and curl) were blocked by this environment's egress gateway (HTTP 403 CONNECT denials confirmed via proxy status log) for every domain attempted (~10 attempts: Substack, Bookseller, PlotProse, Popverse, Pan Macmillan, The Conversation, closeddoorromance.com, etc.), and the Apify browser tool required an approval I couldn't obtain. All quotes and claims therefore come from WebSearch result content (which returned substantial page excerpts) rather than full-page reads; confidence fields are calibrated down accordingly. What worked: trend-roundup queries surfaced consistent, independently corroborating 2026 coverage (yearning/slow-burn demand, femgore rise, romantasy fatigue). What came up dry: direct evidence of specific unmet 'I need a book that feels like X' viral requests (searches returned generic BookTok listicles), and AO3-tag-to-tradpub gap specifics (results covered ship rankings, not underserved tropes) — so I dropped those angles rather than fabricate. Candidates considered and cut: standalone-fantasy demand (real but publishers already responding; weak as a gap), hurt/comfort 'fanfic-coded' books (marketing trend, not a supply gap), special-edition price fatigue (complaint, not book-anchoring). Competitive-landscape checks were done via search (Goodreads lists, Amazon product pages, publisher imprint news) rather than browsing Amazon directly, since Amazon fetches were blocked.

Ran 13 web searches across the assigned angle (AI-era software work, solo consulting/fractional, creator economy, GLP-1 medical realities, special-needs transition, silver-tsunami business exits, AI solopreneurship, career change 50+, HOA/condo boards). Environment constraint: the outbound proxy denied CONNECT to every external host, so all WebFetch/curl attempts returned 403 (verified via proxy status endpoint); the Apify browser tool required an approval that never came. Evidence therefore comes from search-result content that quotes the underlying pages — URLs listed are real results surfaced and read via search, not full-page fetches, and confidence ratings are capped accordingly. Rejected candidates after competitive checks: AI coding agents/agentic engineering books (saturated — Manning, O'Reilly, multiple 2026 titles); GLP-1 maintenance/off-ramp (bigger gap than expected initially, but 'Weightless' (Salas-Whalen, Penguin Random House), 'The Ozempic Revolution' (Sowa, Harper), 'After Ozempic', and a stopping-GLP-1 playbook already cover it plus a cookbook flood); AI-solopreneur (a flood of obviously AI-generated KDP titles — one credits ChatGPT as co-author — makes the niche look open but the discoverability war there is brutal and the claim of a 'quality gap' was not independently verifiable); career-reinvention-at-50-post-AI (pain confirmed but no distinctive evidence of book-gap found in the time available). Dry holes: site:reddit.com queries returned nothing through this search tool, so direct forum-venting quotes could not be collected — that is the main evidence type missing.
